mcqs.question: Which layer forms the foundation of the Android architecture stack?
mcqs.explanation: The Linux Kernel is the bottom-most layer of the Android architecture. It provides hardware drivers, process management, memory management, security, and networking capabilities.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of the Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) in Android?

options.text: To compile Java code to native code
To provide standard interfaces between framework and hardware

mcqs.explanation: HAL provides standard interfaces that expose device hardware capabilities to the higher-level Java API framework, allowing vendors to implement hardware-specific code without modifying the framework.

mcqs.question: Which Android Runtime feature converts bytecode to native machine code at install time?
mcqs.explanation: AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation converts app bytecode (.dex files) to native machine code during installation. This improves runtime performance at the cost of slightly longer install time.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a native C/C++ library in Android?
mcqs.explanation: Activity Manager is part of the Application Framework layer (Java APIs), not the native C/C++ libraries layer. SQLite, OpenGL ES, and WebKit are native libraries.

mcqs.question: ART replaced which earlier runtime environment in Android 5.0?
mcqs.explanation: ART (Android Runtime) replaced the Dalvik Virtual Machine starting with Android 5.0 Lollipop. ART offers AOT+JIT compilation and improved garbage collection compared to Dalvik's JIT-only approach.

mcqs.question: Which Application Framework service manages the lifecycle of activities and the back stack?
mcqs.explanation: The Activity Manager manages the Activity lifecycle, maintains the back stack of activities, and handles transitions between activities in Android applications.

mcqs.question: What security mechanism does the Linux Kernel provide for Android app isolation?

options.text: Each app gets a unique Linux user ID
Apps share a common memory space
All apps run as root user
Apps are isolated using virtual machines

mcqs.explanation: Android assigns each app a unique Linux user ID, leveraging the Linux kernel's user-based permissions model. This ensures apps run in isolated processes and cannot access each other's data without permission.
